Over 1,400 years ago, a book was revealed in the deserts of Arabia — yet millions of people today still look at its verses and feel they speak directly to the realities of our world. 🌍📖

Muslims believe the Qur’an is the literal word of God, revealed to Muhammad in the 7th century. What makes it remarkable to believers is that the Qur’an doesn’t only speak about worship, but also about human nature, corruption, justice, arrogance, and the rise and fall of nations.

Throughout the Qur’an, there are repeated warnings: when societies become arrogant, spread injustice, and forget moral responsibility, their power eventually declines. These are not predictions tied to a single time or place — they are patterns of history.

One example often discussed is found in Surah Al-Isra (Chapter 17), where the Qur’an speaks about cycles of corruption and consequences. For many believers, verses like these show how the Qur’an describes historical patterns centuries before many modern events unfolded.

This is why Muslims see the Qur’an as more than just a historical text. They see it as timeless guidance, a book whose lessons continue to resonate across generations.

A message revealed centuries ago… yet still shaping how millions understand the world today. 🌙

In the field of Consciousness Studies, researchers try to understand one of the deepest mysteries of the human mind: how subjective experience arises from the brain. 

Consciousness is not just about being awake; it includes awareness of thoughts, emotions, sensations, and the sense of “self.” Scientists from neuroscience, psychology, philosophy, and artificial intelligence all approach this question differently. 

For example, neuroscientists study brain activity patterns to see how neural networks produce awareness, while philosophers explore questions like why experiences feel the way they do—often called the “hard problem of consciousness,” a concept popularized by David Chalmers. 

Some theories, such as Integrated Information Theory and Global Workspace Theory, attempt to explain how information in the brain becomes a unified conscious experience. 

Despite decades of research, consciousness remains one of the most complex and fascinating topics in modern science, sitting at the intersection of biology, philosophy, and the study of the mind itself.

What Happened: In a historic cultural initiative on Pakistan Day, the Embassy of Romania in Islamabad unveiled the first-ever professional choral arrangement of Pakistan’s Qaumi Tarāna. The project was developed by the renowned Madrigal – Marin Constantin National Chamber Choir as part of its global cultural diplomacy program.

What you need to know: Originally composed by Ahmed Ghulam Ali Chagla with lyrics by Hafeez Jalandhari, the anthem has been reinterpreted for soprano, alto, tenor, and bass voices while preserving its original composition. The adaptation required careful work due to the anthem’s complex structure and language.

What more: Romania’s Ambassador Dan Stoenescu termed the project a symbol of growing ties between the two countries. The choral score and album have been presented to key Pakistani leaders, including Asif Ali Zardari and Shehbaz Sharif, marking a significant step in cultural cooperation.

في اليابان، يتم استخدام البلاطات الكهروضغطية (piezoelectric tiles) لتحويل حركة المشي إلى كهرباء. هذه البلاطات تلتقط الطاقة الحركية الناتجة أثناء المشي. عندما يمشي شخص فوقها، يتسبب وزنه في انضغاط طفيف في البلاطة، مما يولد ضغطًا ميكانيكيًا (mechanical stress). المادة الكهروضغطية داخل البلاطة تحول هذا الضغط إلى كهرباء. كل خطوة تولد كمية صغيرة من الشحنة الكهربائية (electric charge)، ومع ملايين الخطوات يتم إنتاج كمية كافية من الكهرباء لتشغيل مصابيح LED. في أماكن مثل محطات شيبويا، يساهم حوالي 2.4 مليون خطوة يوميًا في هذا النظام. يمكن تخزين هذه الكهرباء أو استخدامها مباشرة، مما يقلل الاعتماد على مصادر الطاقة التقليدية.

🚨 How Hackers Hide Malware Inside Images & Videos? 🤯💻

Yes, it’s actually possible to embed malicious code inside a normal looking photo or video file. The technique is called Steganography 🕵️‍♂️. In this reel I explain how attackers hide data in plain sight.

👇 Key Concepts Explained in This Reel
• 🧠 Steganography Basics
Images are made up of hundreds of tiny pixels, and each pixel is represented using binary data (1s and 0s).
• 🔬 How Data Gets Hidden
Attackers slightly modify the least significant bits of pixels. This change is so small that the human eye cannot notice it 👀.

• 🖼️ Why Images Are Limited
Images can hide small messages or scripts, but their pixel count limits how much data can be stored.
• 🎥 Why Videos Are Better for Malware
Videos contain thousands of frames, meaning millions of pixels, making them ideal for hiding larger payloads or malicious scripts.

• ⚙️ Common Steganography Tools
Cybersecurity professionals and researchers often demonstrate this using tools like:
🔹 Steghide
🔹 SilentEye
🔹 Online steganography tools

• ⌨️ How Malware Activates
Sometimes attackers hide keyloggers or download scripts that trigger when a victim opens or executes the file.

• 🛡️ Cybersecurity Lesson
Never download random images, videos, or files from untrusted sources. Always verify the source and use security software.

The Netherlands is the tallest country in the world. Massive raw dairy culture. Northern Europe has raw dairy traditions and above-average height across the board. Lactose-intolerant regions with low dairy consumption are consistently shorter. This isn’t a coincidence.
There’s a protein in raw milk called lactoferrin that most people have never heard of. And it has study-backed mechanisms for directly building bone.
Here’s how it works. Your bones are constantly being broken down by osteoclasts and rebuilt by osteoblasts. The balance between these two determines whether you’re growing or shrinking. Lactoferrin tips that balance hard toward building. It reduces RANKL expression which suppresses osteoclast activity. It activates MAPK and Wnt/beta-catenin pathways which drives osteoblast proliferation. It’s not providing calcium passively. It’s actively signaling your body to build more bone.
Then pasteurization destroys 66% of it. Fresh raw milk has 100% lactoferrin intact. Pasteurized milk has 34%. The heat denatures the protein structure completely. You’re drinking what’s marketed as a bone-building food while getting a third of the actual bone-building signal.
And lactoferrin does more than bone. It binds free iron and reduces oxidative stress throughout the body. It disrupts pathogen membranes with antiviral and antibacterial effects. It promotes beneficial gut bacteria and reduces intestinal inflammation. It regulates iron absorption and prevents overload.
One protein. Multiple mechanisms. Destroyed by the processing that was supposedly making your milk safer.
This is why raw milk from healthy grass-fed cows isn’t just nostalgia. The nutritional profile is genuinely different.

What this post is about: This post is a glimpse into the books I study as a cybersecurity professional. These are not motivation books. These are deep technical books that build real skills like exploitation, reverse engineering, secure coding, web security, and red team thinking. Every book here has helped shape how I think, test, and research security in the real world. 1. Practical Binary Analysis – Dennis Andriesse This book teaches how programs actually work at the binary level. It helps you understand disassembly, instrumentation, and Linux internals. Perfect for reverse engineering and malware analysis. 2. Gray Hat C# – Brandon Perry Focused on building real security tools using C#. Great for automation, offensive tooling, and Windows-focused research. Very practical for tool developers. 3. Web Application Security – Andrew Hoffman A modern take on real-world web app attacks and defenses. Explains how vulnerabilities work and how to fix them properly. Ideal for bug bounty and secure development. 4. The Hacker Playbook 3 – Peter Kim A hands-on guide to real penetration testing workflows. Shows how red teams think during real engagements. Best for learning practical attack chains. 5. Hacking: The Art of Exploitation – Jon Erickson A classic book for understanding low-level exploitation. Covers memory, shellcode, and how exploits really work. Builds a strong hacker mindset. 6. Modern C (C23) – Jens Gustedt Teaches modern and safe C programming practices. Very useful for exploit dev, system programming, and research. Strong foundation for low-level security work. These books are slow, hard, and deep. But they build skills that shortcuts never will. #cybersecurity #hacking #infosec

Disclaimer: Most students did not know this happened. Neither does the admin but @dobe_ofc went to Khairpur to check up on the Mir Wah canal and then this happened accidently. 

BUT

Ah yes, Khairpur, my home town, the land of dates, darbars, dusty highways, and a nightlife so subtle it has to be detected with the Faqirs smoking up and reciting Shah Abdul Latif Bhittai and evading university sensors.

Technically, there is no “nightlife” here.
There is only after-Isha energy. I experienced it a lot as a kid. Trust me.

By 4 PM, the city is already in its evening look: half the shops are closed, the other half are pretending to close while actually just switching on Bluetooth speakers and calling it a vibe and chilling on Phatak. Somewhere near a chai dhaba, a corolla with blacked-out windows is playing Paiso Aaaaa at a volume designed to convince the universe that a rave is happening, even though the only audience is three friends who’ve always wanted to settle down in Karachi, one confused rickshaw driver, and a cat.

There are no clubs. There are no bars.
There are only parking lots with ambition.

The dance floor is wherever someone’s cousin parked his Corolla and left the doors open. The DJ is whoever remembered the Wi-Fi password. The light show is provided by a flickering street lamp and someone’s Oppo Android Phone flashlight set to “party mode.” A guy in skinny jeans catching up to 80s fashion is recording Tik Tok like he’s at Tomorrowland, even though the background is a tyre shop and a pani puri stall.

By 11 PM, Falooda that tastes like it was invented five minutes ago. The economy is being powered entirely by DC Office. 

But things are changing! There’s a change coming in and things are about to get lit and wild.

Don’t treat this like a miracle. Most people are carrying 1 to 3 cm of avoidable spinal compression, and once you add posture, pelvic tilt, and compensation into the mix, that can easily become another 1 to 2 cm or more.

These exercises help decompress and rehydrate the spine, improve posture, and reduce the compensations that keep you shorter than you should be. And yes, this can work at any age, because it has not really much to do with it directly, on a fundamental level, it’s mostly intradiscal manipulation driven by mechanobiology.(look up Davis’ Law and the Physical Stress Theory).

Early on, the biggest win is recompression tolerance: your discs and soft tissues get better at handling daily compression, so you stop bleeding height and start reclaiming what poor sleep, bad habits, and modern life were taking from you. But once you’ve built that hold, the decompression work starts to stick. You’re not just getting taller for an hour, you’re gradually shifting what your spine treats as normal, so remodelling can turn into adaptation and your baseline can creep up instead of snapping back.

What you’re seeing in this video is just one way to apply intradiscal manipulation. 🧠 Habits are not built in 21 days. They are automated patterns your brain creates to conserve energy.

⚡ The brain is wired for efficiency. New behaviors are metabolically expensive at first, so resistance is normal. Automation is the brain’s way of lowering that cost.

🔁 Habits form through a loop of cue, action, and reward. Repetition in a consistent context strengthens neural pathways and makes behavior faster and more automatic.

🌲 The more you repeat a behavior, the stronger the circuit becomes. Like carving a path in a forest, clarity comes from consistent use.

🚫 Breaking a habit is not about willpower. You do not erase old pathways, you build new ones that become more efficient through repetition and smart design.

⚠️ Big, rigid goals often fail because they overwhelm the nervous system. When energy drops, the brain defaults to familiar patterns.

📊 Elastic habits work better. Scale the behavior to your energy with Mini, Plus, and Elite options so participation stays consistent.

💪 Make the habit impossible to fail. On low energy days, do the minimum. Consistency wires the brain. Intensity is optional.

🎯 Better habits come from better neural design. Control the cue, reduce friction, lower threat, and work with your nervous system instead of against it.
